name: Buenos Aires
summary: Buenos Aires is generally safe for tourists but requires standard urban precautions. The main risks are petty crimes like pickpocketing and phone theft, particularly in tourist areas and on public transport. Violent crime against tourists is relatively rare, and the city has improved significantly in recent years with declining crime rates.
quickVerdict: Buenos Aires offers a vibrant cultural experience and is safer than many major world cities, but visitors should remain alert. The US State Department maintains a Level 1 advisory for Argentina, and Buenos Aires ranks as the second-safest capital in the Americas with a homicide rate of just 2.5 per 100,000. While petty theft like phone snatching and pickpocketing occurs, especially in crowded areas, violent crime against tourists is uncommon. Most issues are opportunistic rather than aggressive, making awareness and basic precautions your best defense.

neighborhoods.name: Palermo
neighborhoods.description: Trendy and vibrant area with excellent nightlife, restaurants, and shopping. Generally safe day and night with good police presence, though tourist areas can attract pickpockets. Sub-neighborhoods like Palermo Soho and Hollywood are particularly welcoming to visitors.

neighborhoods.name: Recoleta
neighborhoods.description: Upscale and elegant district with European architecture, museums, and cafes. One of the safest areas in Buenos Aires with well-lit streets and regular police patrols. Home to famous cemetery and many luxury hotels.

neighborhoods.name: Puerto Madero
neighborhoods.description: Modern waterfront district with excellent security, well-lit streets, and heavy police presence. Considered one of the safest areas especially at night, with upscale restaurants and hotels. Less culturally authentic but very secure.

neighborhoods.name: Belgrano
neighborhoods.description: Quiet residential neighborhood popular with families and expats. Statistically one of the safest areas with low crime rates. Less touristy but offers authentic local experience with good transport connections.

neighborhoods.name: La Boca
neighborhoods.description: Colorful tourist area famous for Caminito street and tango culture. Safe during daytime on main tourist strip but should be avoided after dark. High risk of theft and muggings if wandering beyond the designated tourist zone.

neighborhoods.name: Retiro
neighborhoods.description: Major transport hub with beautiful plaza areas but higher crime levels near bus and train terminals. Generally safe during day but exercise extra caution at night, especially around transportation stations.

scams.name: Mustard Scam
scams.description: Someone sprays mustard, ketchup, or bird droppings on you while an accomplice offers to help clean it off and steals your belongings in the process.
scams.howToAvoid: If something is spilled on you, politely decline help and move to a safe location. Keep bags secure and don't put them down to clean yourself.

scams.name: Taxi Bill Switching
scams.description: Dishonest taxi drivers take your legitimate large bill, quickly switch it with a counterfeit one, then claim you paid with fake money.
scams.howToAvoid: Use ride-sharing apps like Uber or Cabify, or official radio taxis. Pay with smaller bills when possible and inspect your money before handing it over.

scams.name: Motochorro Phone Theft
scams.description: Thieves on motorcycles snatch phones and bags from pedestrians, especially near busy intersections and while people are distracted.
scams.howToAvoid: Never use your phone within 5 feet of the curb, especially at traffic lights. Keep phones secured in interior pockets and bags worn in front of your body.

scams.name: Fake Emergency Scam
scams.description: Someone approaches claiming to be in distress or needing emergency help, often asking for money or trying to lead you to a secondary location.
scams.howToAvoid: Offer to call emergency services rather than giving money. Don't follow strangers anywhere and be particularly cautious of overly dramatic sob stories.

soloFemale.overview: Buenos Aires is generally safe for solo female travelers, especially compared to other Latin American capitals. The city has a European feel with well-developed infrastructure and women are commonly seen traveling alone. However, street harassment (catcalling) is common and cultural machismo attitudes exist. Most areas like Palermo, Recoleta, and Belgrano feel safe for solo women, but standard precautions are essential.
tips: Stay in well-reviewed accommodations in safe neighborhoods like Palermo, Recoleta, or Belgrano
Dress modestly and avoid flashy jewelry to blend in with locals who tend to dress conservatively
Use ride-sharing apps or official taxis at night rather than walking long distances alone
Ignore catcalling completely - responding usually escalates the situation
Keep your drink in sight at bars and clubs and don't accept beverages from strangers

nightSafety.overview: Buenos Aires has a vibrant nightlife scene that doesn't get started until after midnight, and many areas remain lively and relatively safe due to the crowds. Popular neighborhoods like Palermo and Recoleta feel secure at night with good lighting and police presence. However, some areas become deserted after dark and should be avoided. The key is sticking to busy, well-lit areas and using proper transportation.
tips: Stick to popular nightlife areas like Palermo Soho, Palermo Hollywood, and Recoleta where there are crowds and activity
Avoid deserted areas like parts of Microcentro, San Telmo side streets, and around Retiro terminal at night
Use Uber, Cabify, or official radio taxis for transportation - avoid walking long distances alone
Plan your route home before going out and know exactly how you'll get back to your accommodation
Travel in groups when possible and stay aware of your surroundings even in safe areas

transport.metro: The Subte (subway) is generally safe, efficient, and affordable with good coverage of tourist areas. Watch for pickpockets during rush hours (7:30-9:00 AM and 5:00-6:30 PM) and keep belongings secure. Service runs until late and some lines operate 24/7 on weekends.
transport.rideshare: Uber and Cabify are widely available, safe, and often cheaper than taxis. They're particularly recommended for airport transfers and nighttime travel. The apps work well and you can pay by card, avoiding cash transactions.
transport.taxis: Official black and yellow radio taxis are generally reliable and metered. Avoid unmarked vehicles and be aware of potential scams like bill switching or longer routes. Licensed taxis are safer than street hails, especially at night.
transport.tips: Public transport is extensive and affordable with the SUBE card. Avoid displaying phones or valuables on buses and trains. For late-night travel, rideshares and taxis are safer options than public transport.

customs: Greeting with a kiss on the right cheek is standard, even with strangers - this applies to all genders
Punctuality is flexible - arriving 15-30 minutes late to social events is normal and expected
Dinner is served very late (9-10 PM) and nightlife doesn't start until after midnight - adjust your schedule accordingly

health.overview: Buenos Aires has excellent healthcare facilities, particularly private hospitals and clinics which provide high-quality care. Public hospitals are free but may have long wait times. Tap water is safe to drink throughout the city and food safety standards are generally good in restaurants.
health.water: Tap water in Buenos Aires is safe to drink and meets international standards. Bottled water is widely available if preferred, but unnecessary for health reasons.
health.vaccinations: No specific vaccinations are required for Buenos Aires. Routine vaccinations (MMR, tetanus, flu) should be up to date. Hepatitis A vaccination may be recommended for some travelers.
health.altitude: Buenos Aires is at sea level so altitude is not a concern. The climate is temperate with hot, humid summers and mild winters. Summer temperatures can exceed 35°C with high humidity.

emergency.usEmbassy: US Embassy Buenos Aires: Av. Colombia 4300, Phone: +54 11 5777-4533. Emergency services for US citizens available 24/7.

faq.q: Is Buenos Aires safe for tourists?
faq.a: Yes, Buenos Aires is generally safe for tourists and safer than many major world cities. The US State Department has a Level 1 advisory for Argentina. While petty crime like pickpocketing occurs, violent crime against tourists is rare. Basic urban precautions are sufficient.

faq.q: Is Buenos Aires safe at night?
faq.a: Buenos Aires has vibrant nightlife and many areas like Palermo and Recoleta remain safe at night due to crowds and police presence. Stick to well-lit, busy areas and use ride-sharing apps or taxis for transportation. Avoid empty streets and certain neighborhoods after dark.

faq.q: Is Buenos Aires safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Yes, Buenos Aires is considered one of the safer Latin American capitals for solo female travelers. While street harassment (catcalling) is common, serious incidents are rare. Stay in safe neighborhoods, use ride-sharing at night, and follow standard solo female travel precautions.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Buenos Aires?
faq.a: Avoid La Boca at night and areas beyond the main tourist strip. Exercise caution around Retiro transportation terminals and avoid walking alone in Constitución. Stay away from villa neighborhoods and empty streets after dark, particularly in southern areas of the city.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Buenos Aires?
faq.a: Yes, public transport including the subway and buses is generally safe and efficient. Watch for pickpockets during crowded peak hours and keep valuables secure. For late-night travel, ride-sharing apps or taxis are safer options than public transport.
